title: Are the characters overshadowed by their thematic purposes
display_title: Are the characters overshadowed by their thematic purposes
wikitext:
[]: Are the characters overshadowed by their thematic purposes?             Quote Post by zeaven » Wed Sep 27, 2006 5:30 pm The title is extremely long, and even so, I had to shorten it from "metaphorical contexts". What I'm basically asking is, are the characters themselves not as important as what they represent thematically within the show and/or the subsections of Anno's character they supposedly personify? I ask this, without wanting to go into that issue, because I've heard on many occasions people saying that RahXephon's characters are more well-developed or fleshed out than NGE's. The idea is preposterous to me (but I have never seen RahXephon, so take that assertion with a grain of salt). I know that the characters' metaphorical aspects are very important, but do people just forget all the inter-character intrigue that goes on because they represent something more? It also relates to a discussion I had with a friend who had trouble with the show (not in understanding, but in "liking" it, per se) because every character represents a certain problem that's omnipresent in regards to the "human condition," and the exposure of these things (in media) pained him greatly. It makes no difference that he is one of the majority who is incapable of dealing with his demons; I'm simply wondering if he forgot all about the actual characters when confronted with their "deeper meanings". Sorry if this post is incomprehensible, but I think it makes sense. What do you all think?

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Wed Sep 27, 2006 5:50 pm Characters are not real. But they represent real things. Real aspects of the human condition. I think that characters are nothing more than represenations of certain ideas, themes, and experiences. To ask if characters are overshadowed by thematics is probably an improper question, as characters are naturally a vehicle for other things. Perhaps the question is, do the characters seem too blatant and extreme in what they represent?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Sep 27, 2006 5:55 pm

[]:             Quote Post by zeaven » Wed Sep 27, 2006 6:30 pm Reichu wrote: (an illustrated summary of Sean McCoy's beliefs)

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Wed Sep 27, 2006 6:56 pm zeaven wrote: The characters, by themselves (as, y'know, characters in fiction ) are very interesting. Lots of Bad Things (TM) happen to them. They are cool little spectacles. Do people overlook these incidental things in favor of the bigger picture?

[]:             Quote Post by Mundane » Wed Sep 27, 2006 6:59 pm That is the weirdest penis I have ever seen. Don't you dare try to imply anything from that.

[]:             Quote Post by MaggotMaster » Wed Sep 27, 2006 8:48 pm Mundane wrote: That is the weirdest penis I have ever seen.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Sep 27, 2006 9:33 pm MaggotMaster wrote: I was totally about to post reidick after you said that then I remember that I would probably get banned for that.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Wed Sep 27, 2006 9:40 pm MaggotMaster wrote: I was totally about to post reidick after you said that then I remember that I would probably get banned for that.

[]:             Quote Post by zeaven » Wed Sep 27, 2006 10:55 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: zeaven wrote: The characters, by themselves (as, y'know, characters in fiction ) are very interesting. Lots of Bad Things (TM) happen to them. They are cool little spectacles. Do people overlook these incidental things in favor of the bigger picture?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Sep 28, 2006 12:28 am zeaven wrote: More like that people should be somewhat interested in Asuka getting mind-raped and not just that she represents distancing oneself from a childhood tragedy and acting like an adult in order to forget past traumas.

[]:             Quote Post by zeaven » Thu Sep 28, 2006 1:33 am Reichu wrote: Exactly what is this thread about, again...?

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Sep 28, 2006 1:38 am They're all HEDGEHOGS ! I'd consider it more accurate to say that the characters are used to explore the human condition, flaws and all.

[]:             Quote Post by Kaysow » Thu Sep 28, 2006 5:36 am I'd claim the characters are well-developed and sympathic, and at the same time part of a bigger imagery, in which they are reduced to symbols and puppets, making for the whole dark setting in which all people in this are essentially just tools. It's rather cruel, really, but that's Eva for ya.

[]:             Quote Post by master0rolando » Thu Sep 28, 2006 5:50 am i dont know about all of them being that...maybe...their problems are relatable on the deeper levels Shinji problem = wants people to like him...scared of them not liking him...so he doesnt try to make friends...i think thats a problem weve all had when we were younger... Asuka problem = doesnt want to be babied or rescued and doesnt want to need aynone else...wants to be independant...but cant...again...weve all gone through that one Rei problem = um...this one is harder... shes lonely... she does not feel any self value whatsoever...(because of clones)... that makes me wonder... what would Rei have thought to hear Ritsuko had destroyed all her clones...maybe shed be indifferent but for a split second there...the feeling on being important...irreplacable

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Sep 28, 2006 1:42 pm @zeaven I think the characters are anything but simple archetypes, even though they superficially fit into standard niches (angsty mecha pilot, quiet love interest, raucous interloper); they are complicated and flawed, and feel to me like people @master0rolando Asuka also starts to fall apart badly when her self-image as the best Eva pilot like EVAR! was kicked down by the guy who'd been dragged in off the street just a few weeks before scoring higher that she did. I would also add to the count of her woes alienation pure and simple - the inevitable problem of the exceptionally bright child who lacks a true group of peers.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu Sep 28, 2006 4:20 pm The characters are more than just avatars for their human conditions. Each has their own individual traits distinct from their thematic presence. Shinji plays the cello, Asuka likes emroidery or something, Misato likes cars. They have traits and emotions which flesh them out. It's not blatantly obvious, but we do catch glimpses of their lives outside our immediate perception through their minor actions and words. To be brief, Evangelion characters are more than just one dimensional "emo kids".

[]:             Quote Post by zeaven » Thu Sep 28, 2006 4:44 pm Maybe you guys think I, personally, believe in this notion I'm putting forth, but that's not the case. I was simply wondering if you all had run into people who oversimplified the characters as I suggested. The reception suggests not.

[]:             Quote Post by master0rolando » Thu Sep 28, 2006 5:05 pm Mr. Tines wrote: the exceptionally bright child who lacks a true group of peers.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Fri Sep 29, 2006 12:33 am zeaven wrote: I was simply wondering if you all had run into people who oversimplified the characters as I suggested.
